nativeRange.habitat: Clear, slow- to moderately-flowing mountain streams and rivers with mixed sand-and-rock substrates, leaf litter, submerged driftwood, and shaded forest canopy; found in the Ayeyarwady, Sittaung, Salween, and Ataran river drainages in Myanmar, Manipur state in NE India, and Yunnan Province in China

decorPreferences: driftwood and bogwood pieces with crevices

diet.notes: The Golden Zebra Loach is an omnivore with a strong appetite for snails, readily consuming pest species such as bladder snails and ramshorn snails, as well as other small invertebrates. It also accepts high-quality sinking pellets and wafers, frozen or live bloodworm, Tubifex, brine shrimp, Daphnia, and cyclops. Vegetable matter — blanched cucumber, zucchini, spinach, or melon — rounds out the diet. Do not rely on this species as an algae-eater. Feed sinking foods at or after lights-out to accommodate its crepuscular (dawn- and dusk-active) foraging habits.

goodWith: mid-water community fish of comparable size
other Botia species in sufficiently large groups
avoidWith: dwarf shrimp and small ornamental shrimp (will be eaten)
snails intended for display (will be consumed)
slow-moving long-finned fish such as bettas and fancy guppies (fin-nipping risk)
very small nano fish that may be intimidated
solitary keeping (causes withdrawal and inter-fish aggression)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Botia histrionica (id 24691). Verify availability and license before use.

summary: The Golden Zebra Loach is a boldly patterned shoaling species from the hill streams of northeastern India and Myanmar, reaching up to 13 cm. Valued for its striking yellow-and-black markings and appetite for pest snails, it requires groups of five or more and a mature, well-oxygenated aquarium.
careNotes:
The Golden Zebra Loach (Botia histrionica) is a robust, active botiid from the hill-stream drainages of northeastern India (Manipur) and Myanmar (Ayeyarwady, Sittaung, Salween, Ataran basins), with an outlier population in Yunnan, China. Adults reach 11–13 cm and are intermediate in size within the genus — noticeably larger than B. striata but smaller than the Clown Loach.

Shoaling is non-negotiable: groups of fewer than 5 produce withdrawn, stressed individuals that may harass tankmates, while groups of 5–10 or more display the species’ full behavioural repertoire — complex dominance hierarchies, “shadowing” (mimicking a companion’s movements), and the characteristic “loachy dance” (frantic group swims). Ten or more is ideal in a suitably large aquarium.

Water quality must be high and stable. These fish are intolerant of accumulated organics and should never be introduced to immature aquaria; weekly 30–50 % water changes are advisable. Moderate flow and strong oxygenation replicate the hill-stream conditions of their native range. Temperature 22–28 °C, pH 6.0–7.5, and soft-to-moderately-hard water (gH 2–10) suit the species well.

A minimum footprint of 120 × 45 cm and roughly 280 L is recommended for a group of 5–6; larger is always better. Provide fine sand or smooth pebble substrate at least 5 cm deep, ample driftwood and smooth cave structures, and dense planting with subdued or dappled lighting. Sharp decor edges must be avoided — these fish are notorious for squeezing into tight spaces and can lacerate themselves on rough surfaces. A tightly fitting lid is essential as B. histrionica will jump.

The species is an enthusiastic snail predator and will systematically eliminate bladder, pond, and ramshorn snails from the aquarium — a useful trait for pest-snail control, but incompatible with decorative snail displays or dwarf shrimp. Fin-nipping of slow, long-finned tankmates (bettas, fancy guppies, ornamental cichlids) is a documented risk.

B. histrionica is frequently confused with the closely related B. striata (Zebra Loach), B. kubotai (Polka-Dot Loach), and B. dario (Queen Loach). Juveniles of several Botia species overlap substantially in patterning; adult B. histrionica develop a distinctive golden-yellow ground colour with bold, irregular black cross-bands or reticulated markings that are broader and less regular than B. striata’s fine parallel stripes.
breedingNotes: No confirmed captive spawning has been documented outside of commercial hormone-induced production. Females can be distinguished by a fuller body profile and more rounded snout; males tend toward a more elongated rostrum with fuller lips. Cooler water changes have occasionally triggered courtship displays in aquaria, suggesting a seasonal rainy-season cue. The species is a seasonal egg-scatter spawner in the wild, reproducing during the monsoon in fast-flowing streams. Captive breeding for the hobbyist should be considered not achievable under normal aquarium conditions.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Botia histrionica
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (110–130 mm), temperature (22–28 °C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (36–179 ppm ≈ gH 2–10), minimum tank footprint (120 × 45 cm), native range (Myanmar drainages, Manipur, Yunnan), taxonomy (Botiidae, Cypriniformes), diet, social requirements (groups of 5–6 minimum), and compatibility notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Botia histrionica (species id 24691)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Botiidae, Cypriniformes), maximum size (13.0 cm TL), native range (India and Myanmar, Salween basin), habitat (clear mountain streams, demersal), and IUCN status (Least Concern, 2010). Confirmed benthic/bottom-dwelling behaviour.

additional.name: Loaches Online — Botia histrionica species index
additional.notes: Specialist loach resource. Confirmed pH 6.5–7.2, temperature 25–28 °C, max size ~13 cm, native range (India, Burma, Salween basin), social grouping (3–5+ minimum), and notes on juvenile ID confusion with B. kubotai, B. almorhae, and B. rostrata.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Gold Zebra Loach care guide
additional.notes: Secondary trade source. Confirmed lifespan (~6 years minimum), school size recommendation (6–9+), snail and shrimp predation, fin-nipping risk with bettas, tank minimum (~75 gal / ~283 L), substrate and decor preferences, and sexual dimorphism (females fuller-bodied, males more elongated rostrum).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op Forum — Angelicus botia / golden zebra loach community thread
additional.notes: Hobbyist community discussion corroborating mandatory shoaling behaviour, escape-risk (tight lid requirement), fin-nipping tendencies with long-finned fish, and snail-eating utility. Used as corroborating hobbyist perspective only.

fish.breedingNotes: No confirmed captive spawning in the hobby. Commercially produced in Southeast Asia via hormonal induction. Cooler water changes may trigger courtship displays but full spawning and fry-raising are not achievable under normal home-aquarium conditions.
